full_response:
To compute v(λ)·v(λ) for the given parametric equation v(λ) = (sin(λ²), ln(λ+1), 2λ²-1), we will use the definition of the dot product for vectors in Cartesian coordinates.

Let v(λ) = (v_x(λ), v_y(λ), v_z(λ)).
The dot product of v(λ) with itself is given by:
v(λ)·v(λ) = v_x(λ)² + v_y(λ)² + v_z(λ)²

Step 1: Identify the components of the vector v(λ).
The components are:
v_x(λ) = sin(λ²)
v_y(λ) = ln(λ+1)
v_z(λ) = 2λ²-1

Step 2: Square each component.
v_x(λ)² = (sin(λ²))²
v_y(λ)² = (ln(λ+1))²
v_z(λ)² = (2λ²-1)²

Step 3: Sum the squared components to find v(λ)·v(λ).
v(λ)·v(λ) = (sin(λ²))² + (ln(λ+1))² + (2λ²-1)²

This expression can be written more compactly as:
v(λ)·v(λ) = sin²(λ²) + (ln(λ+1))² + (2λ²-1)²

Final Answer:
\boxed{\text{v}(\lambda)\cdot\text{v}(\lambda) = \sin^2(\lambda^2) + (\ln(\lambda+1))^2 + (2\lambda^2-1)^2}

Confidence: 100%
